title: Infrastructure as Code for Beginners (AWS CDK)
summary: AWS CDK is a Cloud Development Kit that enables Infrastructure as Code in Python (and other languages) to define and deploy AWS resources like DynamoDB, Lambda, and API Gateway programmatically instead of through manual console clicks. The tutorial demonstrates building a serverless REST API using CDK and compares it to CloudFormation and Terraform alternatives.
writeup: A hands-on introduction to AWS CDK that builds a serverless CRUD API (DynamoDB, Lambda, API Gateway) twice: first through the console to illustrate the fragility of manual clickops, then again with Python CDK to show how the same infrastructure becomes a version-controlled, one-command deploy. The through-line is reproducibility: the console works once, but IaC is what lets a second person or a second environment exist without pain.
key_points: Clickops fails the moment a second person or environment enters the picture: a teammate recreates the same table with the wrong partition-key casing, staging silently diverges from production, and a deleted API Gateway resource is unrecoverable because its custom IAM policy was never saved anywhere.

AWS CDK generates CloudFormation templates from real Python code, giving access to loops, conditionals, and autocomplete rather than hand-formatting YAML; it sits above CloudFormation (verbose but foundational) and below Terraform (multi-cloud HCL, but no CloudFormation abstraction for AWS-only work).

The entire serverless stack covered here (Lambda, DynamoDB on provisioned mode, API Gateway) falls within AWS always-free-tier limits: 1 million Lambda requests per month and 25 GB DynamoDB storage, so the tutorial costs nothing to run.

There is a single IAM permission line in the CDK stack that, if omitted, causes every API call to return an access-denied exception; the video flags it explicitly as the error that cost the author two hours in a first project.
Tearing down an environment is a single CDK command that deletes every resource in the correct dependency order, eliminating forgotten DynamoDB tables that quietly accumulate charges.
